statement: Polish Prime Minister Donald Tusk has made several public statements about the need for EU reform to better accommodate smaller member states. As a former President of the European Council (2014–2019), Tusk has deep institutional knowledge of EU decision-making. In 2025–2026, in the context of broader EU reform discussions and the debate about enlargement, Tusk stated publicly that the EU needed to adapt to make membership attractive to countries like Iceland and Norway, and that the EU should listen to smaller states as much as larger ones. These statements were made in the context of the broader EU competitiveness debate (following the Draghi and Letta reports) and the security policy recalibration triggered by Russia's invasion of Ukraine and US policy shifts under Trump's second term.
source_name: Press conference statements — Donald Tusk, PM of Poland (2025–2026)
caveats: The exact wording and context of Tusk's statements about Iceland/Norway should be verified against primary sources (press conference transcripts). Politicians' public statements about enlargement may differ from their actual negotiating positions. Poland has historically been supportive of EU enlargement but its priorities have been Eastern enlargement (Ukraine, Western Balkans) rather than Nordic accession.

related_entries.statement: The Icelandic government announced in early 2026 that a national referendum on resuming EU accession negotiations would be held on 29 August 2026. The referendum is based on a parliamentary resolution (þingsályktunartillaga) submitted by the Foreign Minister. The three coalition parties — Samfylkingin (Social Democrats), Viðreisn (Reform Party), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party) — support holding the referendum. The referendum question concerns whether Iceland should resume the accession negotiations that were suspended in 2013, not whether Iceland should join the EU outright. This is an advisory referendum (ráðgefandi þjóðaratkvæðagreiðsla), as Icelandic constitutional law does not provide for binding referenda on policy questions (the only binding referendum mechanism is under Article 26 for presidential vetoes of legislation).

Iceland applied for EU membership on 16 July 2009, during the aftermath of the financial crisis, under a Social Democrat-led government. Accession negotiations opened in July 2010 and progressed through 33 acquis chapters, of which 27 were opened and 11 provisionally closed by 2013. The most contentious chapters — fisheries (Chapter 13), agriculture (Chapter 11), and free movement of capital (Chapter 4) — remained unresolved. Following the 2013 election, the incoming centre-right coalition suspended negotiations. In March 2015, the government formally asked the EU to remove Iceland from the list of candidate countries. The 2024 Althingi vote for a binding referendum effectively reopened the question without restarting formal negotiations.
